node_summary.purpose: GitHub Actions CI/CD workflow that builds the Jekyll static site using a Docker container whenever code is pushed to main or a pull request targets main branch.
node_summary.behavior: 1. Workflow triggers on push to main branch 2. Workflow triggers on pull request to main branch 3. Job runs on ubuntu-latest runner 4. Repository code checked out via actions/checkout@v4 5. Docker container jekyll/builder:latest pulled and started 6. Repository mounted to /srv/jekyll inside container 7. Output directory mounted to /srv/jekyll/_site 8. Permissions set to 777 recursively on /srv/jekyll 9. Jekyll build command executed with --future flag 10. Build artifacts generated in _site directory

key_data_flows.input: push or pull_request events to main branch
Full repository content via checkout action

side_effects.effect: Docker container downloaded on first run
File permissions modified to 777 in workspace

important_patterns.pattern: Two volume mounts separate source and output directories -v ${{ github.workspace }}:/srv/jekyll (source) -v ${{ github.workspace }}/_site:/srv/jekyll/_site (output)
--future flag includes posts with future dates in build

edge_cases.case: Jekyll not actually used in current site - workflow may be premature
No deployment step - only builds, doesn't deploy to Netlify
chmod 777 is overly permissive but contained in ephemeral container
latest tag may cause unexpected build changes over time

performance.metric: Initial pull of jekyll/builder may take 1-2 minutes
Typical Jekyll builds complete in under 1 minute
performance.concern: No caching of Docker layers or gems between runs

security.item: chmod 777 inside container - isolated from host
latest tag could introduce supply chain risk
security.recommendation: Pin Docker image to specific version tag
Consider adding cache step for faster builds

notes.note: Current site is pure HTML/CSS/JS, not using Jekyll features
Netlify handles actual deployment, this only tests build
Workflow validates that Jekyll can process the site
